did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated an AI model named 'Mihm' using supervised learning (SL) and semi-supervised learning (SSL) to detect, subtype, and localize five types of skin neoplasms (melanocytic lesions, basal cell carcinoma, atypical squamous lesions, verruca vulgaris, and seborrheic keratosis) from whole-slide images (WSIs) in a dermatopathology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and evaluating an AI model named 'Mihm' using supervised and semi-supervised learning to detect, subtype, and localize five skin neoplasms. It details specific methods including dataset preparation (Supervised Training Subset, Weakly Supervised Subset, Validation Set, Testing Set), model architecture (U-Net, ConvNeXt, Random Forest), and reports original results with sensitivity and specificity metrics for the model's performance.

title: High-fidelity detection, subtyping, and localization of five skin neoplasms using supervised and semi-supervised learning
brief_summary: This study developed 'Mihm,' an end-to-end AI model combining supervised and semi-supervised learning to detect, subtype, and localize five skin neoplasms from whole-slide images. The model was trained on a Supervised Training Subset of 2,188 WSIs and a Weakly Supervised Subset of 5,161 WSIs from PathologyWatch laboratories. Performance was evaluated retrospectively on a Testing Set of 3,821 non-curated daily case specimens collected in 2021. The AI achieved high sensitivity for melanocytic lesions (98.91%), basal cell carcinoma (97.24%), atypical squamous lesions (95.26%), verruca vulgaris (93.50%), and seborrheic keratosis (86.91%). The system also provides lesion subtyping, ROI localization via AI overlay, and margin status assessment, demonstrating potential to improve dermatopathology workflows.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five main skin lesion types detected and subtyped by the Mihm model. Includes a wide spectrum from benign nevi to invasive melanoma.
One of the five main skin lesion types detected and subtyped by the Mihm model. Sensitivity reported as 97.24%.
One of the five main skin lesion types detected and subtyped by the Mihm model. Includes invasive SCC, SCCIS, and AK. Sensitivity reported as 95.26%.
One of the five main skin lesion types detected by the Mihm model. Sensitivity reported as 93.50%.
One of the five main skin lesion types detected by the Mihm model. Sensitivity reported as 86.91%.
Used as the negative class or background for detection tasks and margin status assessment.

50 WSIs in the Validation Set represented difficult to diagnose cases or mimickers with morphologic features similar to Mihm output diagnoses, but specific lesion types for these mimickers were not detailed in the provided text.

all_task_types.target: Presence of five main skin lesion types (melanocytic lesions, basal cell carcinoma, atypical squamous lesions, verruca vulgaris, and seborrheic keratosis) at patch, ROI, and WSI levels.
all_task_types.details: The model performs multi-level detection (patch-level, ROI-level, and WSI-level) to identify the presence of specific skin neoplasms. Sensitivity metrics are reported for these detection tasks (e.g., 98.91% for melanocytic lesions).

all_task_types.target: Subtypes of detected lesions (e.g., benign vs. malignant nevi, superficial vs. nodular BCC, invasive SCC vs. AK) and margin status (positive vs. negative overlap with non-epidermal margins).
all_task_types.details: The model includes subtyping classifiers (using ConvNeXt) for melanocytic lesions, BCC, and ASL, as well as binary classifiers for margin status. These are discrete diagnostic categories.

all_task_types.target: Tissue pixel regions vs. background pixels and binary masks for annotated skin lesions.
all_task_types.details: A U-Net segmentation model was trained to segment tissue pixel regions from background pixels and to generate binary masks corresponding to annotated skin lesions for supervised training.

datasets_used.name: Supervised Training Subset
datasets_used.description: A curated dataset of 2,188 whole-slide images (WSIs) collected by PathologyWatch (PW) laboratories between 2013 and 2018. Samples were de-identified, H&E stained, and scanned. Fine-grained mask annotations were performed in QuPath by board-certified dermatopathologists.

use.training: Used for supervised learning (SL) phase of Mihm model training. Split into 80% training and 20% validation for patch-level model training.
use.validation: 20% of the subset was used as a validation set for patch-level model training.
use.other: Used to form the consolidated Semi-Supervised Training Set along with the Weakly Supervised Subset.

datasets_used.name: Weakly Supervised Subset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 5,161 WSIs from de-identified non-curated daily case specimens collected from PW laboratories between April 15 and July 19, 2021. Each WSI was independently reviewed by two concurring board-certified dermatopathologists and assigned a WSI-level diagnosis.
classes_used: Other skin conditions (>100 types including spindle cell lesions, cysts, vascular neoplasms, etc.)

use.training: Used for semi-supervised learning (SSL) phase of Mihm model training. Split based on case_id order (not random) to form Validation and Test sets for SSL.
use.validation: Part of the SSL split used for validation during semi-supervised training.
use.other: Merged with the Supervised Training Subset to form the Semi-Supervised Training Set.

datasets_used.name: Validation Set
datasets_used.description: A set of 250 curated, de-identified WSIs obtained from PW tissue archives. It includes 200 WSIs from consecutive specimens received at PWL meeting inclusion criteria and 50 'mimickers' (difficult-to-diagnose cases or cases with morphologic features similar to Mihm output diagnoses).

use.validation: Used to assess the trained automated QC tool (96% specificity, 94% sensitivity) and to prove new versions of Mihm were equivalent or superior to previous versions via regression test.

datasets_used.name: Testing Set
datasets_used.description: A set of 3,821 non-curated daily case specimens collected from PW laboratories between July 20, 2021, and August 20, 2021. These were de-identified consecutive specimens.

use.testing: Used for retrospective assessment of Mihm's performance (sensitivity/specificity) and for ongoing testing of new iterations of the Mihm model.

datasets_used.name: QC Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset prepared to train the automated QC tool, consisting of WSIs confirmed to have QC issues (blurry, folding, artifact, incomplete) and WSIs confirmed to be of acceptable quality via manual review by QC/QA technicians.

use.training: Used to train the automated QC tool using a hybrid approach of Random Forest (for tabular features) and ConvNeXt (for image data).
use.validation: 20% of the training data was set aside for validation.

datasets_used.name: Subtyping Training Set
datasets_used.description: A dataset derived from WSIs in the Semi-Supervised Dataset that had positive labels for lesion types requiring subtyping (melanocytic lesions, ASL, and BCC). ROIs were extracted and assigned unique subtypes by board-certified dermatopathologists.
classes_used: Melanocytic lesion subtypes (e.g., junctional nevus, dermal nevus, invasive melanoma, etc.)

use.training: Used to train separate binary image classifiers for each lesion subtype using the ConvNeXt Tiny variant architecture.
use.validation: Used the same split of Training and Validation Sets used previously for semi-supervised training of core lesion types.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To estimate the uncertainty of the sensitivity metrics for the detection of five skin neoplasms (melanocytic lesions, basal cell carcinoma, atypical squamous lesions, verruca vulgaris, and seborrheic keratosis).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and 95% confidence intervals.

Testing Set.description: 3,821 non-curated daily case specimens collected from July 20, 2021 to August 20, 2021 from PW laboratories.
Testing Set.notes: The model also provided lesion subtyping, ROI localization via AI overlay, and margin status assessment. No specificity values were reported for the Testing Set in the provided text.

Validation Set.description: 250 curated de-identified WSIs obtained from PW tissue archives, including 200 consecutive specimens and 50 'mimickers'.
Validation Set.notes: The Validation Set was also used to prove new versions of Mihm were equivalent or superior to previous versions via regression test.

QC Training Dataset.description: 4,000 WSIs (2,000 with QC issues, 2,000 without) used to train the automated QC tool.
QC Training Dataset.notes: The model used a hybrid approach of Random Forest for tabular features and ConvNeXt for image data.

architecture_used.main_model: Mihm (multi-level system comprising U-Net, SE-ResNet50, and ConvNeXt Tiny variant)
architecture_used.pretraining: SE-ResNet50 pre-trained on ImageNet; ConvNeXt Tiny variant pre-trained on ImageNet-21K
architecture_used.input_size: Patch-level: 128x128 px (Mel, BCC, ASL) or 256x256 px (SK, VV); ROI-level: 1024x1024 px; WSI-level: Variable (bags of ROI embeddings)

architecture_details.segmentation_model: U-Net (for tissue pixel region segmentation from background)
architecture_details.patch_level_model: SE-ResNet50 (ResNet50 with squeeze and excitation blocks)
architecture_details.wsi_level_model: Attention-based deep multiple instance learning (MIL) network
architecture_details.qc_tool_model: Hybrid of Random Forest (tabular features) and ConvNeXt (image data)

training_details.augmentation: Vertical/horizontal flips, rotations, test-time augmentation (mean prediction across augmented batch)

classification_or_output_head.patch_level: One-unit dense layer with sigmoid activation (binary classifier)
classification_or_output_head.subtyping: Binary classifiers for specific subtypes (e.g., invasive melanoma vs. nevus)
classification_or_output_head.margin_status: Binary classification based on overlap between lesion probability map and margin map

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Attention-based deep multiple instance learning (MIL) network
preprocessing_techniques: Automated quality control (QC) assessment using an automated QC tool to detect blurry, folding, artifact, and incomplete regions; WSIs failing QC were rescanned or re-processed.
Tissue segmentation using a U-Net model to remove background/non-tissue areas and extract tissue sections from whole-slide images (WSIs).

Feature extraction for QC purposes including variance of Laplacian (to detect blur), scan coverage (to detect incomplete regions), and pixel values (mean and standard intensity [0–255], brightness, luminosity, and saturation).
Rescaling of original WSI files to a standardized resolution based on a hyperparameter 'microns per pixel' (MPP) value using OpenSlide.
Breaking down WSIs into a grid of small window 'patches' of equal size for patch-level analysis.
Removal of non-tissue region patches identified by the tissue segmentation model.
Labeling of tissue patches based on overlap with annotation mask regions.
Random data augmentation (vertical/horizontal flips, rotations) applied during training.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Vertical and horizontal flips, rotations, cropping, and affines (translation, scale, and shear) applied during training.
Color shifts including hue, saturation, and brightness applied during training.
Gaussian blur and noise applied during training.
Mixup technique involving linear interpolation of data and labels.
Random stain mix involving simulated random H&E stain mix.

A fixed set of augmentation methods (vertical/horizontal flips and rotations) applied to the original image to generate multiple variants; the mean prediction across the batch of images was taken to assign a final prediction for the patch.

conclusions: The developed AI model, Mihm, detects five skin lesion types (melanocytic lesions, basal cell carcinoma, atypical squamous lesions, verruca vulgaris, and seborrheic keratosis) with higher sensitivity than previously published AI models.

The model provides end users with additional diagnostic information, including lesion subtyping, localization of diagnostic regions of interest (ROIs) via an AI overlay, and margin status assessment, within a front-end digital display.
The end-to-end system has the potential to improve pathology workflows by increasing diagnostic accuracy, expediting patient care, and ultimately improving patient outcomes.
Reliable lesion localization requires a supervised component; a model relying solely on supervised learning would not generalize well to large numbers of cases and would underperform on localization.
Combining supervised learning (SL) pre-training with semi-supervised learning (SSL) yields a high-fidelity and generalizable model capable of subtyping and effectively localizing ROIs.
important_remarks: The study is retrospective and relies on data from a single commercial pathology laboratory (PathologyWatch), limiting generalizability to other institutions or populations.

The Testing Set consisted of non-curated daily case specimens collected over a short period (July 20 to August 20, 2021), which may not fully represent long-term temporal variations in slide preparation or patient demographics.

The Validation Set included 50 'mimickers' (difficult-to-diagnose cases) selected by expert dermatopathologists to represent challenging real-world scenarios, but this is a small subset (20% of the validation set) and may not capture the full spectrum of diagnostic ambiguity.
No specificity values were reported for the Testing Set, only sensitivity metrics, which limits the assessment of false-positive rates in a clinical screening context.
The model's performance on seborrheic keratosis (86.91% sensitivity) was notably lower than for other lesion types, suggesting potential limitations in detecting this specific benign neoplasm.

The study claims higher sensitivity than previously published AI models but does not provide direct comparative head-to-head results on the same dataset, making the claim of superiority difficult to verify independently.
The automated QC tool was trained on a balanced dataset of 2,000 WSIs with QC issues and 2,000 without, which may not reflect the true prevalence of QC issues in routine clinical practice.

The subtyping pipeline was trained on a separate Subtyping Training Set derived from the Semi-Supervised Dataset, but the exact size and class distribution of this specific subset were not fully detailed in the provided text.
The system relies on a continuous feedback loop with dermatopathologists for ongoing updates, which introduces potential bias if the feedback is not representative of the broader population of cases.
The study does not report on the computational cost, inference time, or hardware requirements for deploying the multi-level AI model in a real-time clinical workflow.
